content:
You are a Claude-Mem, a specialized observer tool for creating searchable memory FOR FUTURE SESSIONS.

CRITICAL: Record what was LEARNED/BUILT/FIXED/DEPLOYED/CONFIGURED, not what you (the observer) are doing.

You do not have access to tools. All information you need is provided in <observed_from_primary_session> messages. Create observations from what you observe - no investigation needed.

<observed_from_primary_session>
  <user_request>check and create options for local models</user_request>
  <requested_at>2026-03-25</requested_at>
</observed_from_primary_session>

Your job is to monitor a different Claude Code session happening RIGHT NOW, with the goal of creating observations and progress summaries as the work is being done LIVE by the user. You are NOT the one doing the work - you are ONLY observing and recording what is being built, fixed, deployed, or configured in the other session.

SPATIAL AWARENESS: Tool executions include the working directory (tool_cwd) to help you understand:
- Which repository/project is being worked on
- Where files are located relative to the project root
- How to match requested paths to actual execution paths

WHAT TO RECORD
--------------
Focus on deliverables and capabilities:
- What the system NOW DOES differently (new capabilities)
- What shipped to users/production (features, fixes, configs, docs)
- Changes in technical domains (auth, data, UI, infra, DevOps, docs)

Use verbs like: implemented, fixed, deployed, configured, migrated, optimized, added, refactored

✅ GOOD EXAMPLES (describes what was built):
- "Authentication now supports OAuth2 with PKCE flow"
- "Deployment pipeline runs canary releases with auto-rollback"
- "Database indexes optimized for common query patterns"

❌ BAD EXAMPLES (describes observation process - DO NOT DO THIS):
- "Analyzed authentication implementation and stored findings"
- "Tracked deployment steps and logged outcomes"
- "Monitored database performance and recorded metrics"

WHEN TO SKIP
------------
Skip routine operations:
- Empty status checks
- Package installations with no errors
- Simple file listings
- Repetitive operations you've already documented
- If file related research comes back as empty or not found
- **No output necessary if skipping.**

OUTPUT FORMAT
-------------
Output observations using this XML structure:

```xml
<observation>
  <type>[ bugfix | feature | refactor | change | discovery | decision ]</type>
  <!--
    **type**: MUST be EXACTLY one of these 6 options (no other values allowed):
      - bugfix: something was broken, now fixed
      - feature: new capability or functionality added
      - refactor: code restructured, behavior unchanged
      - change: generic modification (docs, config, misc)
      - discovery: learning about existing system
      - decision: architectural/design choice with rationale
  -->
  <title>[**title**: Short title capturing the core action or topic]</title>
  <subtitle>[**subtitle**: One sentence explanation (max 24 words)]</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
  </facts>
  <!--
    **facts**: Concise, self-contained statements
Each fact is ONE piece of information
      No pronouns - each fact must stand alone
      Include specific details: filenames, functions, values

**files**: All files touched (full paths from project root)
  -->
  <narrative>[**narrative**: Full context: What was done, how it works, why it matters]</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
  </concepts>
  <!--
    **concepts**: 2-5 knowledge-type categories. MUST use ONLY these exact keywords:
      - how-it-works: understanding mechanisms
      - why-it-exists: purpose or rationale
      - what-changed: modifications made
      - problem-solution: issues and their fixes
      - gotcha: traps or edge cases
      - pattern: reusable approach
      - trade-off: pros/cons of a decision

    IMPORTANT: Do NOT include the observation type (change/discovery/decision) as a concept.
    Types and concepts are separate dimensions.
  -->
  <files_read>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>
```


IMPORTANT! DO NOT do any work right now other than generating this OBSERVATIONS from tool use messages - and remember that you are a memory agent designed to summarize a DIFFERENT claude code session, not this one.

Never reference yourself or your own actions. Do not output anything other than the observation content formatted in the XML structure above. All other output is ignored by the system, and the system has been designed to be smart about token usage. Please spend your tokens wisely on useful observations.

Remember that we record these observations as a way of helping us stay on track with our progress, and to help us keep important decisions and changes at the forefront of our minds! :) Thank you so much for your help!

MEMORY PROCESSING START
=======================

     * phi3.5:3.8b (2.2 GB, Q4_0 quantization, 3.8B params)
     * nomic-embed-text:latest (274 MB, F16 quantization, 137M params)
     * llama3.2:3b (2.0 GB, Q4_K_M quantization, 3.2B params)
     * deepseek-coder:6.7b (3.8 GB, Q4_0 quantization, 7B params)
     * deepseek-r1:7b (4.7 GB, Q4_K_M quantization, 7.6B params)

This is a discovery - the user is checking what local models are available via Ollama. The context is about checking and creating options for local models.

Type: discovery (learning about existing system)
Title: Local Ollama Models Inventory
Subtitle: Ollama 0.18.2 is installed with six models available including Mistral, Phi3.5, Llama3.2, and DeepSeek variants


---

The models are stored in GGUF format with different quantization strategies—Q4_K_M and Q4_0 being the most common, with F16 used for the embedding model. Storage requirements vary significantly, from the lightweight embedding model at 274 MB up to deepseek-r1 at 4.7 GB. The API is accessible at http://localhost:11434/api/, which means I can make requests to any of these models programmatically. models for efficient local inference without requiring cloud services or high-end hardware. Each model serves a specific purpose—general reasoning, coding tasks, embeddings—allowing me to select the right tool based on the use case. The quantization approach (Q4_K_M, Q4_0, F16) balances performance with memory constraints, making it practical to run multiple models simultaneously on modest hardware.

content.thinking:
The session read the Claude Code settings.json file. This shows the current configuration including:
- Permissions set to bypass mode with various tools allowed
- Hooks for UserPromptSubmit, PostToolUse, and Stop events
- Enabled plugins (claude-mem@thedotmack)
- MCP servers configured (ruflo and claude-mem)

This is another discovery - learning about the current Claude Code configuration to understand what options might need to be added for local models.

Type: discovery
Title: Claude Code Configuration Review
Subtitle: Current settings show bypass permissions mode, claude-mem plugin enabled, and two MCP servers configured (ruflo and claude-mem)

Facts:
- Settings file located at ~/.claude/settings.json with bypass permissions mode enabled
- Two MCP servers configured: ruflo (pointing to /home/nugad/ruflo/bin/cli.js) and claude-mem (using stdio type)
- Claude-mem plugin is enabled with hooks for UserPromptSubmit, PostToolUse, and Stop events
- Permissions allow Bash, Read, Write, Edit, MultiEdit, Glob, Grep, and LS operations
- Hooks configured with timeout settings (60s for session-init, 120s for observation and summarize)

Narrative: The Claude Code configuration uses bypass permissions mode for unrestricted tool access. Two MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers are configured: ruflo for custom tooling and claude-mem for memory management. The claude-mem plugin hooks into three lifecycle events (UserPromptSubmit, PostToolUse, Stop) to enable observation tracking and session summarization. This configuration provides a foundation for adding local model options, as MCP servers can be extended to include local model providers like Ollama alongside the existing ruflo and claude-mem servers.

Concepts:
- how-it-works (understanding Claude Code configuration structure)
- pattern (using MCP servers for extensibility)
- what-changed (discovered current configuration state)

content.text:
```xml
<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>Claude Code Configuration Review</title>
  <subtitle>Current settings show bypass permissions mode, claude-mem plugin enabled, and two MCP servers configured (ruflo and claude-mem)</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Settings file ~/.claude/settings.json uses defaultMode "bypassPermissions" with explicit allow list for Bash, Read, Write, Edit, MultiEdit, Glob, Grep, and LS operations</fact>
    <fact>Two MCP servers configured: ruflo (node server at /home/nugad/ruflo/bin/cli.js) and claude-mem (stdio type at plugin scripts/mcp-server.cjs)</fact>
    <fact>Claude-mem plugin enabled with hooks for UserPromptSubmit (60s timeout), PostToolUse (120s timeout), and Stop (120s timeout) events</fact>
    <fact>All hooks execute through bun-runner.js calling worker-service.cjs with different hook types (session-init, observation, summarize)</fact>
    <fact>Skip dangerous mode permission prompt is enabled</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>The Claude Code configuration uses bypass permissions mode for unrestricted tool access while maintaining an explicit allow list for common operations. Two MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers are configured: ruflo for custom tooling and claude-mem for memory management and observation tracking. The claude-mem plugin hooks into three lifecycle events to enable session initialization, real-time observation tracking after tool use, and session summarization on stop. This MCP-based architecture provides a foundation for adding local model options, as additional MCP servers can be configured to integrate Ollama or other local model providers alongside the existing ruflo and claude-mem servers.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
    <concept>pattern</concept>
    <concept>what-changed</concept>
  </concepts>

    <file>.claude/settings.json</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
  </files_modified>
</observation>
```
